instruments: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (MLRs), Regulations 8L, 9 and 14A
Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, section 21 (restriction on financial promotion)
Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Financial Promotion) (Amendment) Order 2023
Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Cryptoassets) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/102) -- future superseding perimeter, not yet in force
standing_summary: AML/CFT & Enforcement is the one UK pillar that is fully live today, and it runs on a legally distinct track from the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Cryptoassets) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/102) covered elsewhere in this radar: that instrument does not take legal effect until 25 October 2027, and the FCA is explicit that MLR-registered firms must keep complying with the existing regime 'until the new FSMA regime starts.' The live regime has two independent strands. First, cryptoasset exchange providers and custodian wallet providers carrying on business in the UK must register with the FCA under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017 (Regulations 8L, 9 and 14A) -- the FCA has been AML/CTF supervisor for this sector since 10 January 2020. The FCA's own published determinations table (as at 1 July 2026) shows 408 applications received since supervision began, 388 determined, of which only 67 (17%) resulted in registration, 45 (12%) were rejected, 262 (67%) were withdrawn and 14 (4%) were refused -- meaning roughly five in six determined applications did not end in registration. (This radar cites the FCA's own published proportions rather than the higher 'over 90%' rejection figure sometimes quoted secondhand, which the FCA's current table does not support.) Second, cryptoasset promotions to UK consumers have been within the financial promotions regime since 8 October 2023 under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Financial Promotion) (Amendment) Order 2023; communicating an unlawful cryptoasset promotion is a criminal offence under section 21 FSMA, punishable by up to two years' imprisonment, an unlimited fine, or both, as the FCA set out in its 21 September 2023 final warning letter to the industry ahead of the regime taking effect. The FCA enforces both strands operationally: it maintains a public Warning List flagging unregistered and non-compliant firms; on 10 February 2026 it began High Court action against global exchange HTX for continuing to publish illegal financial promotions on its website and on social media after repeated FCA engagement attempts were ignored; and on 22 April 2026 (updated 18 May 2026) it led a joint operation with HMRC and the South West Regional Organised Crime Unit against eight London premises suspected of unregistered peer-to-peer cryptoasset trading, stating that zero peer-to-peer cryptoasset businesses currently hold FCA registration. On transition, the FCA has stated that being registered under the MLRs 'does not guarantee authorisation under FSMA': from 30 September 2026, firms may submit combined MLR-registration and FSMA-authorisation applications, assessed and determined separately even though submitted together, paying a single fee set at the higher of the two applicable fees; if an MLR application is refused, a firm could still go on to be authorised under FSMA later if it meets the required standards. HMRC's and the National Crime Agency's own enforcement activity in this space is real but is cited here, per this radar's sourcing rules, only through the FCA's own press material describing joint operations, not as an independent source in its own right.

open_items: The FCA's Warning List page (fca.org.uk/consumers/warning-list-unauthorised-firms) returned a Cloudflare bot-challenge response (HTTP 403) to this radar's fetch during this research rather than serving the page directly; its existence and general function are corroborated via the FCA's own 21 September 2023 final warning letter and secondary reporting, but specific current entries, counts, or recent additions to the list could not be independently confirmed against the live page and should be re-verified directly.

The FCA's registration-determinations table (67 registered / 45 rejected / 262 withdrawn / 14 refused, as at 1 July 2026) is a live, rolling figure that the FCA updates periodically; it will already be somewhat dated by the time this card is read and should be re-checked against the current page rather than treated as static.

This pillar state does not cover HMRC's or the National Crime Agency's own cryptoasset enforcement powers or casework in detail: per this radar's sourcing rules, neither body's feed is yet verified as a source, so their activity is reflected here only to the extent the FCA's own press releases describe joint operations (e.g. the 22 April 2026 crackdown, run with HMRC and the South West Regional Organised Crime Unit).

Whether, or how, currently MLR-registered firms will be treated once the FSMA 2026 regime reaches full commencement on 25 October 2027 -- beyond the combined-application mechanics described above -- was not further specified in the FCA guidance found during this research and should be watched for updates as the 30 September 2026 gateway approaches.

The new regulated activities created by the FSMA 2026 Regulations (operating a qualifying cryptoasset trading platform, safeguarding, dealing, arranging deals and staking -- see the Exchanges/VATP and Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillars) will extend the FCA's regulatory perimeter once in force, but do not change the AML/CFT position described here before 25 October 2027.
